steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Martinez and colleagues concluded that S. leucostigma ‘associates’ T. caesius alarm calls with danger. To support that conclusion, a finding must show the birds react specifically to those alarm calls — not to just any disturbance or sound.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘birds froze before any sounds were played’: This shows the birds reacted to the researchers' approach, not to the alarm calls — it undermines the conclusion by suggesting a confounding cause. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘control noise caused no reaction; alarm calls did’: A control test showing that random noise triggered nothing while T. caesius alarm calls triggered a defensive response proves the birds recognized those specific calls — exactly what ‘association’ requires. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘other species also reacted to the alarm calls’: This is about different species, not about whether S. leucostigma selectively recognizes T. caesius calls. It neither supports nor damages the conclusion about this species. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘no variation across different T. caesius individuals’: Consistency across individual callers is interesting, but it does not show that the birds distinguish alarm calls from other sounds. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
